metadata.title: Masculinity, Ritual, and Racialized Status Threat: Examining Mass Shooter Manifestos Using Structural Topic Models

abstract: Both popular media and research often frame mass shootings as an individual issue having to do with mental illness or other individual differences. This work has unfolded in much the same fashion as that on other negative or anti-social behaviors-such as the individual pathologization of suicide or rape. However, what this work has shown empirically is that there are often a set of circumstances that are uniquely social that motivate such actions. Following work in sociology, which offers social psychological and cultural explanations for gun violence, we argue that mass shooter motivations reflect social conditions-especially those that instantiate toxic masculinity, social exclusion, and racism-conducive to these events. This article uses a computational textual modeling approach to analyze the distinct social logics that motivate mass shooters. To do this, we identify a sample of 27 publicly available mass shooter "manifestos," or documents left behind by shooters following their actions. Using topic models, we show that mass shooters exhibit a variety of preoccupations that underlie their actions. While shooters can exhibit a multitude of possible motivations, we find that expressions of masculine overcompensation, ritualistic responses to exclusion, and racialized status threat are prominent features of mass shooter manifestos, corroborating recent sociological explanations of mass shootings.

paragraphs: On June 17, 2015, a shooter 1 walked into Emanuel African Methodist Episcopal (AME; "Mother Emanuel") Church in Charleston, South Carolina. After taking part in a community prayer, the shooter opened fire upon congregants, murdering nine and injuring three others. This assault on a major institution of the Charleston black community was met with several days of public grieving following the initial shock. Beyond the local response, the shooting drew nationwide interest as an example of a broader pattern known as the "mass shooter phenomenon." In the political arena, the phenomenon has reinvigorated public debate about the legality, use, and prevalence of weapons, especially guns.

While the Charleston shooting was an abhorrent act of physical violence, it was also a symbolic racist assault on a key institution of the black community in Charleston as the particular church is one of the oldest predominantly black churches in the United States and the oldest AME church in the South. Further, this church played a key role in organizing the major slave revolt led by congregant Denmark Versey in 1822 and remains a symbol of black pride in the city of Charleston (Keenan and Greene 2019). In his nearly 2,500 word "manifesto," the shooter outlines his support for a broader white supremacist agenda. He concludes by writing:

I have no choice. I am not in the position to, alone, go into the ghetto and fight. I chose Charleston because it is most historic city in my state, and at one time had the highest ratio of blacks to Whites in the country. We have no skinheads, no real KKK, no one doing anything but talking on the internet. Well someone has to have the bravery to take it to the real world, and I guess that has to be me.

The shooter hoped that his actions would incite white supremacist mobilization and further violence against black communities. Like many shooters who leave behind written or otherwise recorded evidence, this letter provides insight into his motivations for the shooting. Such letters suggest a host of social logics that undergird American mass shootings and should be considered as an important source of data for sociologists of mass violence.

This article empirically examines 27 mass shooter letters or "manifestos" using Structural Topic Models (STM) to identify and analyze the distinct social logics that motivate mass shooters. This comparative approach goes beyond extant work that uses content analysis and critical discourse analytical approaches that focus in on as few as a single document (Myketiak 2016;Vito, Admire and Hughes 2018), and compares a larger sample of shooter manifestos. In doing so, we move beyond the pathologization of gun violence and individual determinants approaches that suggest such behavior can be solely attributed to individual differences or mental illness. While we do not disagree that individual factors can play a role in mass shootings, the degree to which these crimes are committed by American men suggests a particular socio-cultural landscape especially conducive to this form of gun violence.

Building on recent sociological work on gun violence and mass shootings, we argue that mass shooter "manifestos" empirically illustrate and expand upon what Bridges and Tober (2019) refer to as a two-part social psychological and cultural process behind this particular type of gun violence. This process attempts to explain why it is overwhelmingly men, and American men in particular, who commit mass shootings. As these events have become more common and garnered significant popular and scholarly attention, it has become evident that in addition to being committed by American men, perpetrators tend to share experiences of gender-specific bullying and perceived gendered threats or exclusion leading to various social aggrievements (Kimmel and Mahler 2003). Social psychological studies of masculine identity threat and overcompensation (Munsch and Willer 2012;Willer et al. 2013) help to explain why men may have more extreme, and perhaps violent, responses to these perceived threats. We also expand this social psychological explanation of mass-shooting events to consider the role of micro-interactional ritual processes that precede mass shootings (Collins 2014). Finally, boys and men today face a shifting socio-cultural landscape with respect to economic change and increasing inclusion and mobilization of historically minoritized groups (people of color, women, LGBTQ groups, etc.) across social spheres. Scholars have argued that these changes have heightened a more general sense of precarity and status threat among white American men, reorienting the cultural meanings and appropriations of guns in the United States toward restoring positions of dominance and power (Bridges and Tober 2019;Carlson 2015aCarlson , 2015b;;Mencken and Froese 2019;Stroud 2012).

Results from our topic modeling analysis reveal that themes related to masculine overcompensation, ritualistic responses to social exclusion rooted in the home, and racialized status threat resulting in aggrieved entitlements are prominent features of mass shooter manifestos. We offer interpretations of these themes illustrated with selected evidence from the text of the manifestos themselves. These findings provide empirical support for a multi-faceted social psychological and cultural process at work in motivating mass-shooting events that extend across multiple documents. They also hold significant implications for moving past individual explanations and developing interventions at social and structural levels. Prior to a discussion of our theoretical framework, findings, and implications, we situate the topic within a brief background on the sociological study of mass shootings.

sections.paper_id: Pfaffendorf et al. - 2021 - Masculinity, Ritual, and Racialized Status Threat Examining Mass Shooter Manifestos Using Structura
Pfaffendorf et al. - 2021 - Masculinity, Ritual, and Racialized Status Threat Examining Mass Shooter Manifestos Using Structura
paragraphs: Mass shootings are commonly defined as events where an attacker or attackers use one or more firearms to strike multiple victims in a confined and populated area, usually choosing victims at random and not involving gang activity, hostage taking, or robberies (Kelly 2012;Lankford 2016aLankford , 2016b)). Such events have gained significant attention in United States in recent years to the extent that the specter of gun violence haunts everyday experiences such as attending concerts and sporting events, going to movie theaters, or even going to school (Altheide 2009;Schildkraut and Nickerson 2020). Efforts to address this anxiety have resulted in changes to the conduct of American life, perhaps most dramatically in the emergence of a multi-billion dollar "Active Shooter Drill" industry administering some type of mass shooter simulation in 95
THEMES IN MASS SHOOTER MANIFESTOS 289

percent of all schools by the 2015-2016 school year (NCES 2019). These drills may serve to capture the zeitgeist of the current era as much as air-raid drills of the 1950s conjure images of Cold War tension and angst.

Trends in the prevalence of mass-shooting events in the United States has been carefully catalogued by scholars who have charted the prevalence in mass-shooting events over recent decades (Blair and Schweit 2014;Bridges and Tober 2019). Not only do mass shooting happen more frequently in the United States than they once did, the United States has become exceptional in terms of the sheer number of mass-shooting events compared to other countries (Bridges and Tober 2019). Lankford (2016aLankford ( , 2016b) ) explores this exceptionalism in a cross-national study covering 171 countries from 1966 to 2012-linking mass shootings to a culture that glorifies gun ownership. Lankford finds that firearm ownership rates were statistically related to the number of mass shooters in a country, even when omitting the United States which has both atypically high numbers of mass shooters as well as unusually high rates of gun ownership. Further, mass shootings have been found to be increasing in lethality over time as shooters learn from previous attacks and spend time planning and amassing more weaponry (Lankford and Silver 2019).

Despite the public attention this phenomenon has garnered, there has been relatively little empirical sociological research focusing on mass shootings and their social determinants. With notable exceptions (Bridges and Tober 2019;Collins 2014;Hawdon and Ryan 2012;Lankford 2016aLankford , 2016b;;Metzl and MacLeish 2015;Muschert 2007), sociologists have neglected the topic while research in other disciplines has been able to offer explanations for the extreme acts of violence committed by mass shooters. Psychologists, for instance, have focused on the extent to which mass shooters might have serious, oftentimes undiagnosed mental illnesses (Dutton et al. 2013;Ferguson et al. 2011;Lankford 2018;Swanson et al. 2015). This focus on individual determinants, however, has cast offenders as lone wolves. That is, actors who undertake their activity mostly alone, and on their own terms, defying social norms and logics. Yet, attackers commonly have a community of co-conspirators, oftentimes behind screens in online communities or other clandestine organizations-communities with shared norms and logics that have been adopted by those like the Charleston shooter who saw himself as acting on behalf of a white supremacist community.

Following research on the social underpinnings of mass shooter violence (Newman et al. 2008), we argue that a sociological perspective, rather than a sole focus on individual or psychological factors, can illuminate broad sociocultural logics that are common across many mass-shooting events and how they stem from societal issues at large. In the section that follows, we build upon previous research to offer explanations for mass-shooting events in the United States rooted in social context. Throughout, we apply these explanations to mass shooter manifestos, which, as we illustrate, offer key insights into the contextual factors that motivate mass shooters.

sections.paper_id: Pfaffendorf et al. - 2021 - Masculinity, Ritual, and Racialized Status Threat Examining Mass Shooter Manifestos Using Structura
sections.header_raw: Mass Shootings as Social Psychological and Cultural Process
sections.header_norm: mass shootings as social psychological and cultural process
paragraphs: Recent sociological research on gun violence provides important insights into the particular social dynamics that underlie mass shootings in the United States. In particular, Bridges and Tober's (2019) two-pronged social psychological and cultural explanation for mass shootings committed by American men offers a useful starting point for examining this phenomenon in general and the sociological significance of mass shooter manifestos specifically. Thus, we begin by briefly outlining the main tenets of this framework. Within this discussion, we also incorporate several related literatures (such as that on ritual processes and social exclusion) to advance a more holistic understanding of the social psychological and cultural processes involved in motivating this type of violence.

At both the social psychological and cultural levels, research and theorizing have linked mass shootings in the United States-and increasing gun desirability and ownership more generally-to American men's perceived positions of precarity (Bridges and Tober 2019;Carlson 2015aCarlson , 2015b;;Stroud 2012). However, responses to this precarity can be analytically classified across these two levels. At the social psychological level, experimental research has been used to link certain compensatory behaviors-including aggression and violence-among American men to identity threat, and masculinity threat in particular. Meanwhile, micro-level interactionist research reveals how mass shooter motivations hinge on processes of ritual building rooted in social exclusion and bullying. At the cultural level, research on guns and violence suggests a shifting ideological terrain under which men --especially white American men -might turn to extreme acts of violence like mass shootings. In the following section, we discuss each of these social psychological and cultural processes and their implications for understanding the social determinants of mass shootings. Throughout this discussion, we link these theoretical paradigms to existing empirical work on mass shooter manifestos to guide our expectations.

Mass-Shooting Events and Masculine Overcompensation. Part of the relationship between mass shootings and masculinity can be understood through the logic of identity theory and gender theory on masculine identity threat. Identity theories such as identity control theory (Burke 1991;Burke and Stets 2009) explain generally why individuals might enact compensatory responses to a perceived threat to their identity. Identity control theory holds that identities are stabilized via a cyclical relationship between an identity THEMES IN MASS SHOOTER MANIFESTOS 291 standard, behavior, and situational feedback. That is, individuals interpret feedback from others that allows them to assess how well their behavior matches the cultural meanings associated with a salient and situationally relevant identity. When this feedback suggests that behavior does not match the identity standard, it creates a deflection where individuals attempt to bring their behavior back in line with that standard. In some cases, this entails more extreme enactments of behaviors associated with a given identity (Glick et al. 2015;Willer et al. 2013).

Studies of men and masculinities provide insights into why men might exhibit heightened compensatory responses to gender identity threat compared to women and people of other gender identities. Raewyn Connell's (1995) theorizing on masculinities maintains that hegemonic masculinity exists as an idealized, culturally exalted form of masculinity that has historically supported the hierarchical dominance of certain men over others. In the United States, hegemonic masculinity is associated with several common characteristics, including strength, certain kinds of violence, aggression, emotional stoicism, and competition (Pfaffendorf 2017). Given the (real or perceived) power and dominance associated with the successful embodiment of hegemonic masculinity, the stakes of meeting this ideal are relatively high for men. As such, there tends to be less flexibility within what is construed as an acceptable masculine gender identity compared to a feminine one. This can be seen in the relentless and persistent policing of femininity in boys and men (Diefendorf and Bridges 2019; Pascoe 2007) at the same time as there is growing acceptance of certain kinds of gender fluidity, such as masculine styles among girls (as exemplified in cultural normalization of the "tomboy").

Along these lines, Willer and colleagues (2013) find support for what they refer to as the "masculine overcompensation thesis," or the idea that men are particularly sensitive to gender identity threat and often respond with displays of hypermasculinity. In their study, men whose masculinity was experimentally threatened were more likely to support violent or oppressive institutions such as war, homophobia, and male supremacy (while women showed no effects when their femininity was threatened). In an earlier study, Munsch and Willer (2012) also find that men whose masculinity was threatened were more likely to blame women portrayed in vignettes depicting sexual coercion-increasing men's support of, and perhaps participation in, the subjugation of women.

While laboratory studies like these demonstrate that masculinity threat can shape political and cultural attitudes that support masculine domination, they are also important for examining how such responses might translate into action among mass shooters (Bridges and Tober 2019). Although experiences such as homophobic bullying that cause boys and men to perceive masculinity threat are endemic to many US institutions like schools (Pascoe 2007), gender-specific bullying has become an all but defining feature of mass shooters (Kimmel and Mahler 2003), those deemed "at risk" of becoming mass shooters (Reuter-Rice 2008), and those who express interest in mass shootings (Raitanen et al. 2017). In the face of masculinity threats like these, scholars find that violence and aggression are some of the most readily available tools men have at their disposal for (re)signifying a masculine self (Connell 1995;Schrock and Schwalbe 2009) and restoring dominance amid masculinity crises (Messerschmidt 1999). The gendered nature of identity threat discussed here and its propensity to spur extreme, violent reactions allows for a social psychological explanation for why mass shooters are almost entirely men.

Themes related to threatened or "fragile" masculinities have also been found in empirical research on mass shooter manifestos. For instance, Myketiak's (2016) takes a critical discourse analysis approach to a single manifesto left behind by a shooter in Isla Vista, California in 2014 and finds that the shooter was motivated by fragile masculinity and positioned himself in an antagonistic struggle against women and minority men. The shooter's manifesto highlights his failed romantic pursuits juxtaposed with his expectations for these pursuits. The text of the manifesto reveals a desire for retribution against both women as and minority men, who the shooter believes women prefer (Myketiak 2016, p. 2).

Mass Shootings as Anomic Rituals. As part of his more general theory of violence, Randall Collins (2014) builds upon Muschert (2007) to make distinctions between those who commit terrorist acts, those who commit general violence, and those who become mass shooters (Collins 2014). Collins connects mass-shooting activity with the micro-interactionist tradition in sociology, writing that mass shooters "build elaborate fantasy backstage lives that becomes a substitute for successful interaction rituals on the front stage" (Collins 2014, p. 412). Collins describes a common type of shooter who creates symbols and rituals to make sacred their backstage experience. By analyzing the broad meanings that shooters attribute to their act, Collins provides sociologists one piece of a puzzle, namely, that one can consider mass shooters as acting within a coherent relational field of social activity marked by social inclusion and exclusion.

Collins explains that shooters "obsess" over their potential action, creating sets of rituals and developing a deep backstage life in reference to their troubled worldview. Focusing on evidence from a high school shooting in Paducah, Kentucky in 1997, Collins argues that the shooter developed an obsessive fascination with violent backstage counter-rituals after being systematically excluded from the popular social groups in school. This social exclusion often took the form of bullying through physical attacks and social ostracization that resulted in emotional damage that was repressed by the shooter. The shooter Collins analyses make attempts to join alternative communities ("Goths"), but was, eventually, rejected by this community as well. Collins describes the shooting as the result of a series of exclusionary events marked by a deepening retreat of the shooter into his internal and increasingly violent backstage life (Collins 2014).

Patton (2012) illustrates how some of these ritual processes play out in online videos produced by mass shooters, which serve as a space of identity construction. Shooters pursue individuation strategies by self-staging videos that are simultaneously reproducing elements associated with well-known mass shooters, but also emphasizing distinctive motivations and often describing themselves as free-thinking individuals (Patton 2012, p. 212). Like Collins (2014), Patton (2012) notes that shooters carefully craft these images to frame themselves as iconic anti-heroes of the era as a retreat from daily lives often characterized by negative experiences of social exclusion and interpersonal domination.

Comparative empirical research on mass shooters that goes beyond a single case finds that social exclusion and bullying may be an important precipitating factor leading to mass shootings, especially school shootings. Research on the self-perceptions of mass shooters reveal that a majority of shooters describe themselves as socially marginalized (Newman et al. 2008), an isolation that is linked directly to the violent act. The US Secret Service commissioned "Safe School Initiative" finds social marginalization and bullying to be a factor in over two-thirds of the cases analyzed (Vossekuil et al. 2002). Meanwhile, the most comprehensive study on the topic finds that a wide range of schoolbased conflicts preceded mass-shooting events, ranging from physical bullying, romantic rejection, and conflict with teachers (Sommer et al. 2014). Given the prominence of social exclusion in previous shootings, it is likely shooters will reference exclusion in their manifestos. While we expect that shooters may reference explicit episodes of social rejection, bullying, and instability, we also consider the possibility that the language of exclusion may take the form of attention seeking given that research has demonstrated that fame-seeking behavior is a common component of mass shooters' motivations (Bushman 2018;Lankford 2016aLankford , 2016b;;Lankford and Silver 2020).

Shifting Landscapes of American Opportunity. Social psychological processes shed light on why men may exhibit extreme-and perhaps violentresponses to gendered identity threat in addition to the violent backstage counter-rituals mass shooters exhibit in response to systematic social exclusion. However, Bridges and Tober (2019) argue that these individual responses must be understood in terms of socio-cultural context and widespread cultural beliefs about American society to explain why it is American men in particular who are disproportionately more likely to commit mass shootings compared to men in other countries. Carlson's (2015aCarlson's ( , 2015b) ) research illustrates the importance of the US context for understanding these issues. Her work on gun carriers in Michigan suggests that American gun violence reflects a deeper cultural problem that is not easily explained by simple matters of gun control. Rather, gun desirability has increased in tandem with a shifting social landscape that the men in her study lamented as a decline of "Mayberry" America, an idealized nation characterized by economic prosperity, safety, and cohesive communities. The imagined loss of Mayberry-which Carlson attributes to the disappearance of a strong manufacturing economy and a destabilization of the nuclear family and rigid gender roles-has transformed, for many American men, the cultural meaning of guns into a "sensible, morally upstanding solution" (Carlson 2015a:9) to problems associated with socioeconomic decline. While one of these problems may be the perceived threat of crime, men also use guns to shore up the loss of core components of American masculinity that define men as breadwinners. When breadwinning roles are out of reach, men appropriate guns to highlight their roles as masculine protectors, which functions as an alternate route of attaining gendered status.

Even amidst decline, rising wage inequality, and diminished possibilities for economic mobility (Bjorklund and Breiger 2020;Chetty et al. 2017;Piketty 2014), belief in upward mobility and opportunity remains embedded in American aspirations (Younis 2019). These beliefs are central to Donald Trump's "Make America Great Again" campaign, which mobilizes optimism in the resurgence of Middle America. However, what made this imagined period "great" for some was the systematic exclusion of others (Bridges and Tober 2019). As Carlson (2015aCarlson ( , 2015b) ) suggests, the strong heteronormative foundations and urban flight that made Mayberry conceivable ensured that white, middle-class men were its primary beneficiaries.

Recent work on patterns of gun ownership suggests that these changes might motivate certain men to move beyond the use of guns for armed self-defense (Yamane 2017) and toward a more generalized defense of the ideals that once upheld their dominance. Patton (2012) illustrates how some of these ritual processes play out in online videos produced by mass shooters, which serve as a space of identity construction. Shooters pursue individuation strategies by self-staging videos that are simultaneously reproducing elements associated with well-known mass shooters, but also emphasizing distinctive motivations and often describing themselves as free-thinking individuals (Patton 2012, p. 212). Like Collins (2014), Patton (2012) notes that shooters carefully craft these images to frame themselves as iconic anti-heroes of the era as a retreat from daily lives often characterized by negative experiences of social exclusion and interpersonal domination.

Comparative empirical research on mass shooters that goes beyond a single case finds that social exclusion and bullying may be an important precipitating factor leading to mass shootings, especially school shootings. Research on the self-perceptions of mass shooters reveal that a majority of shooters describe themselves as socially marginalized (Newman et al. 2008), an isolation that is linked directly to the violent act. The US Secret Service commissioned "Safe School Initiative" finds social marginalization and bullying to be a factor in over two-thirds of the cases analyzed (Vossekuil et al. 2002). Meanwhile, the most comprehensive study on the topic finds that a wide range of schoolbased conflicts preceded mass-shooting events, ranging from physical bullying, romantic rejection, and conflict with teachers (Sommer et al. 2014). Given the prominence of social exclusion in previous shootings, it is likely shooters will reference exclusion in their manifestos. While we expect that shooters may reference explicit episodes of social rejection, bullying, and instability, we also consider the possibility that the language of exclusion may take the form of attention seeking given that research has demonstrated that fame-seeking behavior is a common component of mass shooters' motivations (Bushman 2018;Lankford 2016aLankford , 2016b;;Lankford and Silver 2020).

Shifting Landscapes of American Opportunity. Social psychological processes shed light on why men may exhibit extreme-and perhaps violentresponses to gendered identity threat in addition to the violent backstage counter-rituals mass shooters exhibit in response to systematic social exclusion. However, Bridges and Tober (2019) argue that these individual responses must be understood in terms of socio-cultural context and widespread cultural beliefs about American society to explain why it is American men in particular who are disproportionately more likely to commit mass shootings compared to men in other countries. Carlson's (2015aCarlson's ( , 2015b) ) research illustrates the importance of the US context for understanding these issues. Her work on gun carriers in Michigan suggests that American gun violence reflects a deeper cultural problem that is not easily explained by simple matters of gun control. Rather, gun desirability has increased in tandem with a shifting social landscape that the men in her study lamented as a decline of "Mayberry" America, an idealized nation characterized by economic prosperity, safety, and cohesive communities. The imagined loss of Mayberry-which Carlson attributes to the disappearance of a strong manufacturing economy and a destabilization of the nuclear family and rigid gender roles-has transformed, for many American men, the cultural meaning of guns into a "sensible, morally upstanding solution" (Carlson 2015a:9) to problems associated with socioeconomic decline. While one of these problems may be the perceived threat of crime, men also use guns to shore up the loss of core components of American masculinity that define men as breadwinners. When breadwinning roles are out of reach, men appropriate guns to highlight their roles as masculine protectors, which functions as an alternate route of attaining gendered status.

Even amidst decline, rising wage inequality, and diminished possibilities for economic mobility (Bjorklund and Breiger 2020;Chetty et al. 2017;Piketty 2014), belief in upward mobility and opportunity remains embedded in American aspirations (Younis 2019). These beliefs are central to Donald Trump's "Make America Great Again" campaign, which mobilizes optimism in the resurgence of Middle America. However, what made this imagined period "great" for some was the systematic exclusion of others (Bridges and Tober 2019). As Carlson (2015aCarlson ( , 2015b) ) suggests, the strong heteronormative foundations and urban flight that made Mayberry conceivable ensured that white, middle-class men were its primary beneficiaries.

However, white masculine privilege has become more visible and tenuous today with increasing inclusion of historically marginalized groups (women, people of color, LGBTQ individuals, etc.) and mobilization against the inequities that sustained these divisions (Bridges Forthcoming; Bridges and Tober 2019:9). Recent work on patterns of gun ownership suggests that these changes might motivate certain men to move beyond the use of guns for armed self-defense (Yamane 2017) and toward a more generalized defense of the ideals that once upheld their dominance. For instance, in their examination of American gun culture, Mencken and Froese (2019) find that guns operate as a means by which white men who experience economic precarity and social alienation restore a sense of individual power and moral purpose. Other studies establish

an explicit empirical link between economic and racial threat among whites and gun desirability (Costanza et al. 2020;Costanza and Kilburn 2004).

Linking gender differences with economic and racialized status threat, Warner (2020) finds that non-gun owning men are motivated by a constellation of cultural anxieties, such as concerns about (white) American identity and resistance to immigration, in their likelihood to consider owning a gun. Indeed, Stroud (2012) refers to contemporary patterns of gun appropriation as a form of "gendered racism," finding in interviews with men who are gun carriers that "simply coming into contact with black men induces a desire to carry" (234). Intersectional scholarship demonstrates how responses like this are a partly a product of repeated exposure to controlling images (Collins 2004) that associate black masculinity with violence and criminality-constructing black men as "threat[s] to white society" (Collins 2006:75; see also Dow 2016; Carlson 2018). Incidentally, controlling images, such as the "thug," for which black men are held to account also disallow them from appropriating guns on the same terms as their white counterparts (Carlson 2018). These studies are consistent with a wider body of work showing rising support for a range of punitive policies and social control measures aimed at restoring white (masculine) dominance (Behrens, Uggen, and Manza 2003;Bjorklund et al. 2018;Brown 2013;Fox 2010;King and Wheelock 2007).

Further, research on mass shooter manifestos demonstrates that these status threat responses based on intersecting social inequalities should be a prominent feature that recurs across multiple documents. In their thematic content analysis of the Isla Vista shooter document, Vito and colleagues (2018) demonstrate that the shooter's fragile masculinity was rooted in a sense of "aggrieved entitlement" based on his social location as a white man-despite his self-admitted lacking of other socially desirable characteristics related to physical capacity and sexual prowess (Vito et al. 2018). Here, the shooter's whiteness specifically informs his expectations for sexual relationships and experiences grievances directed toward ethnic and racial minorities for their perceived experience in this field.
sections.preview_head: Recent sociological research on gun violence provides important insights into the particular social dynamics that underlie mass shootings in the United States. In particular, Bridges and Tober's (2019) two-pronged social psychological and cultural explanation for mass shootings committed by American men offers a useful starting point for examining this phenomenon in general and the sociological significance of mass shooter manifestos specifically. Thus, we begin by briefly outlining the main tenets of this framework.

paragraphs: Given the empirical and theoretical literature explored in depth above, we suggest three separate propositions that serve to organize our formal expectations for our empirical analysis. Each proposition is rooted in a specific social logic that may motivate mass shooters. Following the statement of these propositions, we move to a description of our research strategy and empirical results.
Proposition A: Elements related to shooters' fragile masculinity will be a prominent theme across mass shooter manifestos.

Proposition B: Elements related to shooters' social exclusion and/or bullying will be prominent themes across mass shooter manifestos. Proposition C: Elements related to shooters' status threat will be a prominent theme across mass shooter manifestos.

sections.paper_id: Pfaffendorf et al. - 2021 - Masculinity, Ritual, and Racialized Status Threat Examining Mass Shooter Manifestos Using Structura
Pfaffendorf et al. - 2021 - Masculinity, Ritual, and Racialized Status Threat Examining Mass Shooter Manifestos Using Structura
paragraphs: This paper makes use of a custom dataset of mass shooter manifestos that were compiled in 2020. Manifestos are the direct words of mass shooters themselves produced in advance of their shooting and can take several forms of media-handwritten, typed, or digitally recorded. These manifestos serve as primary source evidence of a shooter's stated motivations for their actions, while at the same time offering sometimes lengthy insights into their worldviews, political or religious beliefs, and personal life. We aggregated the manifestos for this project from multiple sources. For school shooters, we downloaded manifestos directly from the website www.schoolshooters.info which serves as an archive of international school shooting incidents. Additionally, collected non-school shooter manifestos from hard news sources or personal Web sites were available. A number of high profile shooters were excluded from our final analysis for several reasons-either because they did not leave a manifesto (e.g., the Pulse Nightclub shooter), their manifestos were never made public (e.g., the Columbine High School shooters), or their manifestos were composed of a majority of symbols or equations that would not yield interpretable results that could be compared with the other documents in our sample (e.g., the Aurora Colorado Theater shooter). If manifestos were typed or in .pdf format, we used optical character recognition software to convert them into a plain text format. In the event that the documents were handwritten or in video format, the authors transcribed these manifestos for the first time. These documents were compiled into a .CSV file. In total, 27 publicly available mass shooter manifestos were compiled for this project.

Table 1 provides the descriptive characteristics of the sample for this project. Unsurprisingly, an overwhelming majority of school shooters included in this sample were men (N = 26). Similarly, a majority (62.9%) of the sample were non-Hispanic white, while the remaining 37.1 percent of the sample were Asian-American (N = 3), Hispanic (N = 3), black, (N = 1), Native American (N = 1), and Middle Eastern (N = 1). All of the mass shootings analyzed in this study took place in the United States. There were a wide variety of targets chosen by the shooters including high schools, colleges, elementary schools, grocery stores, non-profit organizations, and churches, though high schools were the most frequent target of the shooting (N = 14). The earliest shooting

sections.paper_id: Pfaffendorf et al. - 2021 - Masculinity, Ritual, and Racialized Status Threat Examining Mass Shooter Manifestos Using Structura
paragraphs: The goal of this study is to evaluate the latent social themes embedded in these manifestos to generate a greater understanding of mass shooter motivations, and in particular, identify the extent to which mass shooter manifestos exhibit pronounced language related to fragile masculinity, social exclusion or bullying, and status threat based in social status loss. To do this, we make use of STM which is a probabilistic topic modeling strategy for estimating latent themes within text (Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2014). STM is an automated text analysis procedure that assumes a collection of texts (in this case shooter manifestos) is built from a non-random combination of topics and words. The algorithm collects topics that are built from word co-occurrences that highlight patterned themes within texts that are indicative of underlying structure. In other words, STM draws out non-random collections of words that are useful for interpreting the types of themes, dimensions, or topics that comprise documents. Like all topic modeling procedures, STM helps to reduce interpretive bias by allowing for prominent themes to emerge from documents that may or may not be expected by analysts. However, unlike other topic modeling procedures, STM has a unique suite of packages that allow for data-driven decision making in how models are specified. This informed our choice to go with this particular variant of topic modeling procedure. In combination with traditional content analysis procedures like deep reading, topic modeling offers a powerful computational tool that can lead to more complete understandings of textual data by reducing bias and permitting reproducibility (Nelson 2020). We supplemented our STM procedure with a deep reading of each mass shooter manifesto to accurately assign labels to terms.

To conduct our STM analysis, we utilized the stm package in R (Roberts et al. 2014). The stm package in R offers a native suite of functions that obviate the need to specify various parameters in advance, a difficulty that is present in other topic modeling methods such as latent Dirichlet allocation LDA (Gaby and Caren 2016;Kinney, Davis, and Zhang 2018). First, we prepared the texts for analysis by pre-processing the data in a number of ways. Common stopwords were removed (e.g., "the," "and," "or") that are not useful for interpretation and often confound analysis. Additionally, we created a custom set of stopwords to specifically remove date and time language (e.g., "january," "monday," "clock"). This was done because while these words may be meaningful in other types of analysis, they contributed a disproportionate amount of statistical noise to the STM output in this project because often manifestos took 298 JESSICA PFAFFENDORF ET AL.

By semantic coherence, the searchK function provides a score that indicates how frequently words co-occur with each other across topics (Mimno et al. 2011). By exclusivity, the search K function provides a score that

Together, both metrics offer researchers the ability to judge the quality of resulting topics a priori, offering a heuristic for the relative success of human interpretation. Given the low N of the sample, we chose to prioritize semantic coherence in selecting model specifications. Common stopwords were removed (e.g., "the," "and," "or") that are not useful for interpretation and often confound analysis. Additionally, we created a custom set of stopwords to specifically remove date and time language (e.g., "january," "monday," "clock"). This was done because while these words may be meaningful in other types of analysis, they contributed a disproportionate amount of statistical noise to the STM output in this project because often manifestos took 298 JESSICA PFAFFENDORF ET AL.

indicates the frequency of independent words across topics (Roberts et al. 2014). The resulting output suggested that retrieving 6 topics would provide the best balance of both metrics with deference to semantic coherence. 2 These 6 topics were assigned custom thematic labels based on their associated words and used to evaluate the propositions described above.
sections.preview_head: The goal of this study is to evaluate the latent social themes embedded in these manifestos to generate a greater understanding of mass shooter motivations, and in particular, identify the extent to which mass shooter manifestos exhibit pronounced language related to fragile masculinity, social exclusion or bullying, and status threat based in social status loss. To do this, we make use of STM which is a probabilistic topic modeling strategy for estimating latent themes within text (Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2014). STM is an automated text analysis procedure that assumes a collection of texts (in this case shooter manifestos) is built from a non-random combination of topics and words.

sections.paper_id: Pfaffendorf et al. - 2021 - Masculinity, Ritual, and Racialized Status Threat Examining Mass Shooter Manifestos Using Structura
paragraphs: Figure 1 presents the top 6 topics generated from the STM output. Words with capitalized letters refer to the custom labels that the researchers assigned to each topic. Likewise, the words with lower case letters correspond to the most highly associated terms populating each topic. While making claims about the relative importance of each topic to understanding mass shooter rationales is beyond the scope of this paper, it is important to note that these topics are ordered from most prevalent to least prevalent across the corpus in the figure among prominent topics. This means that the topic "APOLOGIES" represents the most prominent theme across all documents included in the sample and "HOMELIFE/SOCIAL EXCLUSION" is the least prominent theme across all documents included in the sample. Despite this comparative note, all topics are sufficiently prominent to appear among the 6 topics identified by the modeling procedure. While other themes such as those related to political ideology certainly appear in specific manifestos, they do not co-occur across documents to such an extent that they would appear in our topics.

Immediately, this figure reveals supporting evidence of the initial proposition that mass shooters express attitudes that reflect fragile masculinities and status-based threats in their manifestos. The topic "STATUS THREATS" reveals themes that cut across race and gender through the terms "white," "black," "favorite," and "women" that paint a stark picture of motivations stemming from perceptions of racial/ethnic and gendered challenges to their identity and social position. Terms associated with this topic likely came from prominent documents such as the Charleston shooter's letter that used explicit white supremacist language, yet these terms were sufficiently prominent across documents that terms related to race were found to occur across documents. Additionally, the topic "FRAGILE MASCULINITY" which contains the terms "kill," "men," and "girls," and notably "rejected" reflects additional gendered dimensions that suggest a link between the stated motivations of mass shooters and masculine overcompensation. As previous research on fragile masculine identities and mass shootings describes, narratives of romantic or sexual

THEMES IN MASS SHOOTER MANIFESTOS 301 1475682x, 2021, 2, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/soin.12409 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [02/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
rejection by women are a key component to the types of aggrieved masculinities that are common to mass shooters' narratives (Myketiak 2016).

Exemplifying "FRAGILE MASCULINITY" AND "STATUS THREAT," one shooter writes, "My whole life has been one lonely enterprise. One loss after another. And here I am, 26, with no friends, no job, no girlfriend, a virgin. I long ago realized that society likes to deny people like me these things. People who are elite, people who stand with the gods." In this passage, the individual articulates a sense of entitlement to women, sex, attainment, and friendship based purely on his self-described "elite" position. The shooter goes on to attribute his lack of success with women to racialized perceptions of the dating field, writing that "I'm not gay, girls just didn't want me. As I said before they went for the thug blacks." Expressing discontent and aggreivement with his lack of success in dating, he shifts the blame for his own perceived failiures by invoking the controlling image (Collins 2004) of the "thug" --situating black men as the problem. It should be noted that this shooter identified as mixed race, suggesting that the white supremacist motivations that sometimes precede mass shooting events can transcend certain racial boundaries, perhaps mapping onto colorism or other differences instead.

Others echoed this language noting discontent with interracial relationships based in overt white supremacist, anti-black, and sexist beliefs. In this vein, one shooter articulates how white women in interracial relationships are "victims" that require "saving": "I have noticed a great disdain for race mixing White women within the White nationalists community, bordering on insanity. . .. These women are victims, and they can be saved." These racist sentiments are bound up with other controlling images like the "black male rapist" that have historically been mobilized to disenfranchise and disempower black communities using the foil of "pure" white femininity that requires perpetual protection (Collins 2004, Davis 1981). This language in addition to the topics related to "FRAGILE MASCULINITY" and racialized "STATUS THREAT" underscore previous findings that have noted a gendered racism in American gun culture (Bridges and Tober 2019; Stroud 2012), here taken to the extreme. These topics are also consonant with more general findings in contemporary gun research that suggest that American men turn to guns in the face of perceived failure to live up to cultural expectations-be they related masculinity, racial dominance, or economic standing (Carlson 2015a(Carlson , 2015b;;Costanza et al. 2020;Costanza and Kilburn 2004;Mencken Froese 2019;Warner 2020).

The results also support a micro-interactionist interpretation of mass shootings as a reflection of personalized "backstage worlds" constructed as ritualized reactions to isolation (Collins 2014). A number of topics in the above figure center explicitly on themes related to exclusion and social alienation. The topics "APOLOGIES" which includes the terms "love," "hate," "sorry," and "help," "ANGER" which includes the terms "fuck," "fucked," and "weak," and "HOMELIFE/SOCIAL EXCLUSION" which includes the terms "real," "knew," "suicide," "began," and "home" all suggest isolation as a common experience of mass shooters. These topics also suggest that the source of isolation may begin at home, rather than at school or another site-at least in terms of the ways that mass shooters typically represent their social lives.
A shooter that exemplifies this logic of isolation writes:

Hey Everybody!! I feel rejected, rejected, not so much alone, but rejected. I feel this way because the day-to-day treatment I get usually it's positive but the negative is like a cut, it doesn't go away really fast. I figure by the time you guys are reading this I'll probably have done what I told EVERYONE I was going to do. THEMES IN MASS SHOOTER MANIFESTOS 303

Here, the shooter interprets a lack of attention from others at school as a type of rejection. He seeks to punish those who have ignored him and those who did not make a sufficient effort to understand him. While many shooters focus on social interactions at school, other shooters focus on their home life. In narratives that focus on the home, shooters either express deep regrets for the pain they know their actions will cause their families or reflect on painful experiences in the home that, from their perspective, precipitated the event. In each of these situations, shooters discuss their anticipated actions as events that grew out of configurations of intimate social interactions-events that had been the product of obsessive planning and ritualistic preoccupation.

Finally, a number of topics are associated with the sources or sites of isolation and the pragmatic planning of the shooting itself, which are pertinent to both understanding the motivations for mass shootings and also the specific sites. The topics "HOMELIFE/SOCIAL EXCLUSION" points to the home as a core site that exacerbates the problems that mass shooters link to their anticipated actions. While it would be inappropriate to shift blame from the actual shooter to others, this topic highlights how the home can become a powerful space of socialization that can prompt reactionary behavior. This is further supported by the topic "APOLOGIES". In some respects, these topics reveal the conflicted emotional space that mass shooters operate within that can be at once aggrieved and resentful as well as cognizant of responsibility-so much so that they take space in their manifestos to offer apologies to parents or significant others. Similarly, the topic "PLANNED SHOOTING" which includes the terms "shoot," "today," and "date" suggests-unsurprisingly-that much language of mass shooter manifestos is dedicated to a description of the planned action as well as the details about when and where the action will take place. This language is potentially of concern to researchers seeking to observe patterns in an effort to intervene in planned mass-shooting events, though late-stage interventions such as these do little to address the underlying social psychological and cultural processes that seem to be conducive to mass-shooting events.

sections.paper_id: Pfaffendorf et al. - 2021 - Masculinity, Ritual, and Racialized Status Threat Examining Mass Shooter Manifestos Using Structura
paragraphs: This study takes a computational text analytic (STM) approach to studying mass shooters and the recorded evidence that they leave behind. We situate our empirical analysis within three propositions drawn from the existing theories of masculinity, ritual, and status that reflect a social-contextual-rather than individualistic-account of the motivations of mass shooters. Making use of a large sample of manifestos, we find substantial support for our theoretical expectations. Most notably, we find support for both Proposition A, which suggested that a topic related to fragile masculinity would be prominent across mass shooter manifestos, as well as Proposition C, which suggested that a topic related to shooters' status threat would be prominent across mass shooter manifestos. These topics appear as clear and prominent themes across documents. On the other hand, we find more qualified support with regard to Proposition B, which suggested that a topic related to social exclusion and bullying would be prominent in these texts.

First, we find that masculinity threat rooted in compensatory responses to romantic and sexual rejection is a prominent theme across mass shooter manifestos. This offers confirmation of our theoretical expectations in Proposition A. Based on shooters' stated motivations, their violent actions can be linked to expressions of compensatory masculinity in an effort to redress feelings of inadequacy and rejection (Bridges and Tober 2019). In addition, we find that shooters often discuss the motivations for their shootings in terms that reveal feelings of anxiety toward racial and ethnic minorities, specifically in regard to romantic relationships, as we anticipated in Proposition C. For both of these topics, shooters discuss these grievances openly in their manifestos-often using racist and sexist language-some making explicit references to social and political causes rooted in white supremacy and anti-blackness. These findings corroborate extant literature on the gendered and racialized motivations of mass shooters across a broad sample of manifestos (Myketiak 2016;Vito et al. 2018). Further, our findings are in line with contemporary gun research that suggests that American men turn to guns in situations of precarity or perceived failure (Carlson 2015a(Carlson , 2015b;;Mencken and Froese 2019), and suggests that these responses to precarity are gendered and racialized (Stroud 2012). Specifically, shooters mobilize racist and mysoginist tropes or controlling images akin to the "thug" and the "black male rapist" (Collins 2004, Davis 1981) to explain their aggreivement, and sometimes to justify their actions. This study is also one of the first to offer formal comparative empirical support for recent theoretical frameworks that offer social explanations of mass-shooting events (Bridges and Tober 2019).

As we discussed, we find only qualified support for Proposition B that laid out our expectations that bullying and social exclusion would be a prominent topic in our corpus. While terms related to fragile masculinity and status-based threat were apparent across the documents in our sample, mass shooters were more reluctant to reference specific incidents of bullying or social exclusion related to peers at school. Instead, they tended to focus on the breakdown of close social relationships in the home, such as those with parents and siblings. On the one hand, this is surprising given the prevalence of school bullying and exclusion in other research on mass shootings (Newman et al. 2008;Vossekuil et al. 2002). However, this may reflect Collins' (2014) finding that mass shooters often repress social exclusion and bullying-retreating to an inner backstage. Shooters are humiliated by their experiences with their peers and rarely report these in their manifestos, at least not to the extent that they appear in our topic models as prominent topics.

This study takes a computational text analytic (STM) approach to studying mass shooters and the recorded evidence that they leave behind. We situate our empirical analysis within three propositions drawn from the existing theories of masculinity, ritual, and status that reflect a social-contextual-rather than individualistic-account of the motivations of mass shooters. Making use of a large sample of manifestos, we find substantial support for our theoretical expectations. Most notably, we find support for both Proposition A, which suggested that a topic related to fragile masculinity would be prominent across mass shooter manifestos, as well as Proposition C, which suggested that a topic related to shooters' status threat would be prominent across mass shooter manifestos. These topics appear as clear and prominent themes across documents. On the other hand, we find more qualified support with regard to Proposition B, which suggested that a topic related to social exclusion and bullying would be prominent in these texts.

First, we find that masculinity threat rooted in compensatory responses to romantic and sexual rejection is a prominent theme across mass shooter manifestos. This offers confirmation of our theoretical expectations in Proposition A. Based on shooters' stated motivations, their violent actions can be linked to expressions of compensatory masculinity in an effort to redress feelings of inadequacy and rejection (Bridges and Tober 2019). In addition, we find that shooters often discuss the motivations for their shootings in terms that reveal feelings of anxiety toward racial and ethnic minorities, specifically in regard to romantic relationships, as we anticipated in Proposition C. For both of these topics, shooters discuss these grievances openly in their manifestos-often using racist and sexist language-some making explicit references to social and political causes rooted in white supremacy and anti-blackness. These findings corroborate extant literature on the gendered and racialized motivations of mass shooters across a broad sample of manifestos (Myketiak 2016;Vito et al. 2018). Further, our findings are in line with contemporary gun research that suggests that American men turn to guns in situations of precarity or perceived failure (Carlson 2015a(Carlson , 2015b;;Mencken and Froese 2019), and suggests that these responses to precarity are gendered and racialized (Stroud 2012). Specifically, shooters mobilize racist and mysoginist tropes or controlling images akin to the "thug" and the "black male rapist" (Collins 2004, Davis 1981) to explain their aggreivement, and sometimes to justify their actions. This study is also one of the first to offer formal comparative empirical support for recent theoretical frameworks that offer social explanations of mass-shooting events (Bridges and Tober 2019).
